    "license": "http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/","articleSection": "Research Article","name": "Board Gender Diversity and Investment Efficiency: evidence from UK Private Firms","headline": "Board Gender Diversity and Investment Efficiency: evidence from UK Private Firms","abstract": "This paper examines the relationship between private firm investment efficiency and board gender diversity in the UK from 2006 to 2019. The findings indicate that board gender diversity has a positive impact on private firm investment efficiency, with the effect primarily driven by reductions in overinvestment rather than underinvestment. Contrary to the critical mass hypothesis, even the presence of a single woman director is associated with improved investment efficiency, and this positive effect becomes more pronounced as women's representation increases. Further analysis reveals that the effect of women directors is stronger in financially constrained firms and more pronounced in non-family firms. The findings support regulations to increase the representation of women directors in private firms.",
    "description": "This paper examines the relationship between private firm investment efficiency and board gender diversity in the UK from 2006 to 2019. The findings indicate that board gender diversity has a positive impact on private firm investment efficiency, with the effect primarily driven by reductions in overinvestment rather than underinvestment. Contrary to the critical mass hypothesis, even the presence of a single woman director is associated with improved investment efficiency, and this positive effect becomes more pronounced as women's representation increases. Further analysis reveals that the effect of women directors is stronger in financially constrained firms and more pronounced in non-family firms. The findings support regulations to increase the representation of women directors in private firms.",

Uncheck the box to turn MathJax off. This feature requires Javascript. Click on a formula to zoom.</span></span></span></div><div class="hlFld-Fulltext"><div id="abstractId1" class="hlFld-Abstract"><h2 class="section-heading-2" id="abstract">ABSTRACT</h2><p class="last">This paper examines the relationship between private firm investment efficiency and board gender diversity in the UK from 2006 to 2019. The findings indicate that board gender diversity has a positive impact on private firm investment efficiency, with the effect primarily driven by reductions in overinvestment rather than underinvestment. Contrary to the critical mass hypothesis, even the presence of a single woman director is associated with improved investment efficiency, and this positive effect becomes more pronounced as women's representation increases. Further analysis reveals that the effect of women directors is stronger in financially constrained firms and more pronounced in non-family firms. The findings support regulations to increase the representation of women directors in private firms.</p></div><div class="abstractKeywords"><div class="hlFld-KeywordText"><div><p id="kwd-title-d69031680e161" class="kwd-title">Keywords: </p><ul aria-labelledby="kwd-title-d69031680e161"><li><a href="/keyword/investment+efficiency" class="kwd-btn keyword-click">investment efficiency</a></li><li><a href="/keyword/UK+private+firms" class="kwd-btn keyword-click">UK private firms</a></li><li><a href="/keyword/board+gender+diversity" class="kwd-btn keyword-click">board gender diversity</a></li><li><a href="/keyword/critical+mass" class="kwd-btn keyword-click">critical mass</a></li><li><a href="/keyword/investment+efficiency" class="kwd-btn keyword-click">investment efficiency</a></li><li><a href="/keyword/UK+private+firms" class="kwd-btn keyword-click">UK private firms</a></li><li><a href="/keyword/board+gender+diversity" class="kwd-btn keyword-click">board gender diversity</a></li><li><a href="/keyword/women+directors" class="kwd-btn keyword-click">women directors</a></li><li><a href="/keyword/critical+mass" class="kwd-btn keyword-click">critical mass</a></li></ul></div></div></div><div class="abstractKeywords"><div class="hlFld-KeywordText"><div><p id="kwd-title-d69031680e182" class="kwd-title">JEL: </p><ul aria-labelledby="kwd-title-d69031680e182"><li><a href="/keyword/codes%3A+G30" class="kwd-btn keyword-click">codes: G30</a></li><li><a href="/keyword/G31" class="kwd-btn keyword-click">G31</a></li><li><a href="/keyword/J16" class="kwd-btn keyword-click">J16</a></li></ul></div></div></div><div id="embedded-pdf-target" class="with-border"></div><div class="pb-dropzone" data-pb-dropzone="contentNavigationDropZoneAbs"><div class="widget gql-content-navigation none  widget-none" id="2ffb43c7-15dc-4c79-9e29-8e4bed9b3d1e">

            <div class="widget-body body body-none "><div class="ajaxWidget" data-ajax-widget="gql-content-navigation" data-ajax-widget-id="2ffb43c7-15dc-4c79-9e29-8e4bed9b3d1e" data-ajax-observe="true">

        </div>
    </div></div><div class="NLM_sec NLM_sec_level_1" id="S001"><h2 class="section-heading-2" id="d1e192">1.  Introduction</h2><p>Understanding the factors that determine private firm resource allocation is crucial for the sustained success of private businesses, which constitute a significant proportion of developed and developing economies worldwide. In 2022, UK private firms made up over 99% of all businesses and contributed more than 50% to the annual gross domestic product (GDP) (Department for Business Energy and Industrial Strategy, <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0035" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2022</a></span>). Given the economic importance of private firms to the UK economy, this study examines the impact of board gender diversity on the investment efficiency of private firms, with a specific focus on capital investment decisions.</p><p>Prior studies offer empirical evidence concerning the influence of boardroom gender diversity on investment efficiency using publicly listed firms (Almor et al., <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0006" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2022</a></span>; Chen et al., <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0025" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2016</a></span>; Farooq et al., <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0042" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2023</a></span>). However, there is a lack of evidence on how boardroom gender diversity influences capital allocation decisions in private firms that typically operate with weaker governance, less transparency, different ownership structures, and, in some cases, token appointments of women directors (Ingram et al., <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0059" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2010</a></span>; Langli & Svanström, <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0070" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2014</a></span>; Sealy et al., <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0091" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2009</a></span>). These institutional and governance differences may significantly impact the investment efficiency of private firms.</p><p>Private firms also differ from public firms in several fundamental ways that directly shape investment behavior. They operate under more flexible legal and disclosure requirements (Ball & Shivakumar, <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0013" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2005</a></span>; Tyrowicz et al., <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0101" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2020</a></span>), face reduced external monitoring, adopt governance structures distinct from those of public firms (George, <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0049" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2005</a></span>; Ingram et al., <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0059" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2010</a></span>), and are typically owner-managed, illiquid, and characterized by concentrated ownership (Asker et al., <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0010" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2015</a></span>). UK evidence further shows that private firms always pay more for acquisitions than listed firms (Saunders & Steffen, <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0090" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2011</a></span>) and exhibit significantly higher leverage (Brav, <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0018" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2009</a></span>), underscoring the distinct financial behavior of UK private firms. International evidence suggests that private firms generally exhibit lower investment sensitivity (Gilje & Taillard, <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0050" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2016</a></span>; Mortal & Reisel, <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0082" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2013</a></span>), pay out fewer dividends (Michaely & Roberts, <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0080" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2012</a></span>), and face greater constraints on financing and acquisitions (Maksimovic et al., <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0077" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2013</a></span>). These distinctions contribute to the lack of understanding of the role of women directors in private companies. The objective is to bridge this knowledge gap by examining the impact of board gender diversity on the effectiveness of corporate investments using data from UK private firms.</p><p>Given the characteristics of private firms and the increased representation of women on boards (Ball & Shivakumar, <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0013" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2005</a></span>; Hampton-Alexander Review team, <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0055" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2019</a></span>), exploring gender diversity in private firms is both informative and timely. Diversity theory suggests that gender-diverse boards enhance investment efficiency through improved cognitive diversity, information exchange, and monitoring effectiveness (Bantel & Jackson, <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0014" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>1989</a></span>; Chen et al., <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0023" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2019</a></span>; Cox & Blake, <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0030" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>1991</a></span>; Watson et al., <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0105" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>1993</a></span>). Women directors also tend to exercise more independently-minded characteristics (Chen et al., <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0023" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2019</a></span>) and bring unique communication and working styles to boardroom responsibilities (Daily & Dalton, <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0032" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2003</a></span>), thereby constraining managerial opportunism and contributing to more effective decision-making. These mechanisms may be especially salient in private firms, where illiquidity and concentrated ownership reduce external discipline, forcing reliance on board oversight to guide investment decisions (Asker et al., <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0010" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2015</a></span>). Critical mass theory (Kanter, <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0067" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>1977</a></span>) suggests that the benefits of gender diversity may become more pronounced when at least three women are present on the board.</p><p>Analysing 50,201 firm-year observations covering 13,757 UK private firms from 2006 to 2019 reveals a robust, statistically significant positive correlation between gender diversity and the effectiveness of corporate investments, with a notable influence on mitigating overinvestment issues. Moreover, the results indicate that even one woman director can enhance investment efficiency. This effect is twice as pronounced when there are three or more women on the board. Likewise, the effect of curbing overinvestment issues is more pronounced for boards with at least three women directors. The findings are robust concerning endogeneity.</p><p>Our study provides numerous valuable contributions to the existing scholarly research. First and foremost, the research enhances the literature on corporate investment efficiency by specifically analyzing private firms, a setting that remains understudied due to data limitations and the dominance of research on public firms. Second, this paper broadens the application of diversity theory beyond public-firm settings and re-examines the relevance of critical mass theory in the context of private firms. While existing public-firm evidence emphasizes agency-based explanations (Farooq et al., <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0042" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2023</a></span>), our analysis draws on diversity theory to highlight how cognitive diversity, enhanced information exchange, and stronger monitoring may shape investment outcomes in private firms. Furthermore, we extend the theoretical discussion by examining whether meaningful influence may arise even when women's representation is limited, given the unique governance characteristics of private firms. Third, the dataset encompasses all sizes of private firms, imposes no restrictions on asset size, and maintains transparency in the selection of both board characteristics and financial data, thereby effectively mitigating exclusion and selection biases. Lastly, the study offers valuable insights for regulators considering implementing board gender diversity quotas as an effective governance measure.</p><p class="last">Subsequent sections of this study are structured in the following manner. Section <a href="#S002" data-behaviour="scroll" data-behaviour-ref="#S002" data-behaviour-callback="focus" data-behaviour-callback-target="#S002">2</a> evaluates the existing literature and develops the hypotheses. Sample selection details, research design, and methodology are given in Section <a href="#S003" data-behaviour="scroll" data-behaviour-ref="#S003" data-behaviour-callback="focus" data-behaviour-callback-target="#S003">3</a>. Section <a href="#S004" data-behaviour="scroll" data-behaviour-ref="#S004" data-behaviour-callback="focus" data-behaviour-callback-target="#S004">4</a> reports and discusses the regression results. Finally, Section <a href="#S005" data-behaviour="scroll" data-behaviour-ref="#S005" data-behaviour-callback="focus" data-behaviour-callback-target="#S005">5</a> concludes the study, discussing theoretical insights and practical suggestions.</p></div><div class="NLM_sec NLM_sec_level_1" id="S002"><h2 class="section-heading-2" id="d1e311">2.  Background, literature review, and hypotheses development</h2><div class="NLM_sec NLM_sec_level_2" id="S002-S2001"><h3 class="section-heading-3" id="d1e315">2.1.  Background</h3><p>UK private firms play a dominant role in the UK economy (Sattar et al., <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0088" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2022</a></span>), making the study of investment efficiency particularly important. The number of private firms in the UK increased steadily up to 2019, followed by a temporary decline due to COVID-19 pandemic and subsequent recovery in more recent years (Department for Business & Trade, <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0036" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2025</a></span>; Department for Business Energy and Industrial Strategy, <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0034" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2019</a></span>). At the beginning of 2025, approximately 5.7 million private companies generating £5,52 trillion of revenues (Department for Business & Trade, <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0036" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2025</a></span>). Over the last 15 years, private firms in the UK have experienced significant growth. As of the beginning of 2019, approximately 5.9 million private companies generating £4.15 trillion of revenues (Department for Business Energy and Industrial Strategy, <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0034" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2019</a></span>). The sector offers a sizable, comprehensive sample of businesses of all sizes, as shown in <button class="ref show-table-fig-ref" data-id="F0001" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-popup-event-type="fig">Figure 1</button>.
</p><div class="figureView"><div class="short-legend"><p class="captionText"><span aria-level="3" role="heading"><span class="captionLabel">Figure 1. </span></span> Number of UK private business firms: 2006–2025. Source: Business Population Estimates, UK government (Department for Business & Trade, <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0036" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2025</a></span>; Department for Business Energy and Industrial Strategy, <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0034" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2019</a></span>).</p></div><a href="#" class="thumbnail" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-popup-event-type="fig" data-id="F0001"><img id="d1e352" src="/cms/asset/fde18a79-7fc4-4452-9ba1-81e7f66f9d50/raie_a_2622079_f0001_oc.jpg" loading="lazy" height="252" width="500" alt="Figure 1. Number of UK private business firms: 2006–2025. Source: Business Population Estimates, UK government (Department for Business & Trade, Citation2025; Department for Business Energy and Industrial Strategy, Citation2019)."></a><div><button class="show-full-size btn btn-sm" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-id="F0001" data-popup-event-type="fig">Display full size</button></div></div><p></p><p>The regulatory and reporting environment for UK private firms differs markedly from that of listed firms. The UK Companies Act requires all registered companies, including private and public companies, to prepare and report annual financial statements in accordance with accounting standards. Moreover, in the UK, tax regulations are the same for private and public businesses, and firms meeting a certain threshold must get their financial statements audited(Ball & Shivakumar, <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0013" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2005</a></span>; Brav, <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0018" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2009</a></span>). Thus, UK private firm data are highly available and reliable.</p><p>Although the UK does not impose mandatory gender quotas on corporate boards, regulators strongly support gender-diverse companies. A UK government-backed commission led by Lord Davies recommended that FTSE 100 boards voluntarily have at least 25% women representation by 2015 (Davies, <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0033" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2011</a></span>). Subsequently, the Companies Act 2006 required all UK firms to disclose the proportion of women on their boards in their annual strategic reports (UK Public General Acts, <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0103" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2016</a></span>). In 2016, the new Disclosure and Transparency Rules (DTRs), published by the UK Financial Conduct Authority (UK FCA), mandated all UK companies listed on European Union markets to disclose corporate diversity policies in companies’ corporate governance statements or to provide justification for not doing so (UK Financial Conduct Authority, <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0102" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2016</a></span>). These initiatives have resulted in a level of female board representation that surpasses that of many other prosperous nations with similar non-binding regulatory frameworks. By 2019, FTSE 100 firms had 32.4% of women on their boards, and FTSE 250 firms reported 29.6% of women in corporate boardrooms (Hampton-Alexander Review team, <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0055" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2019</a></span>). Internationally, as of 2022, the UK was ranked seventh among European countries in terms of the representation of women on corporate boards, with a margin of only 5% less than the top-ranking country, France (Gender Statistics Database, <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0048" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2022</a></span>). Furthermore, the presence of women on the boards of UK-listed firms has been steadily increasing, ranking the UK eighth among European countries based on growth rates from 2006 to 2019 (Gender Statistics Database, <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0047" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2019</a></span>).</p><p class="last">In contrast, gender diversity in UK private firms remains more limited and less regulated. <button class="ref show-table-fig-ref" data-id="F0002" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-popup-event-type="fig">Figure 2</button> illustrates the proportion of women directors on boards, using the sample data collected for this research. Notably, the representation of women directors in UK private firms peaked in 2012, which may be attributed to the implementation of the UK Corporate Governance Code in 2011. This code required listed firms to report diversity policies starting from October 2012. Although this regulation specifically targeted listed firms, the widespread promotion of gender diversity across the UK market likely increased the emphasis on women directors on boards in private firms as well. After 2012, there is a slight decline over the following seven years. The reasons for the decline could be attributed to the lack of gender diversity regulations requiring them to disclose details about their firm's gender diversity practices and policies. Overall, the data reveal a notable upward trend in the percentage of women directors within the boardrooms of UK private firms over the 13-year study period.
</p><div class="figureView"><div class="short-legend"><p class="captionText"><span aria-level="3" role="heading"><span class="captionLabel">Figure 2. </span></span> Annual average proportion of women directors in sampled UK private firms.</p></div><a href="#" class="thumbnail" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-popup-event-type="fig" data-id="F0002"><img id="d1e395" src="/cms/asset/dda2e420-c6cd-4f64-9fbf-cf58cc4416c2/raie_a_2622079_f0002_oc.jpg" loading="lazy" height="258" width="500" alt="Figure 2. Annual average proportion of women directors in sampled UK private firms."></a><div><button class="show-full-size btn btn-sm" data-behaviour="show-popup" data-id="F0002" data-popup-event-type="fig">Display full size</button></div></div><p></p></div><div class="NLM_sec NLM_sec_level_2" id="S002-S2002"><h3 class="section-heading-3" id="d1e398">2.2.  Literature review and hypotheses development</h3><p>With the growing representation of women on corporate boards, research focused on comprehending the impact of gender diversity at the board level has become increasingly popular. According to Erhardt (<span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0040" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2003</a></span>) diversity theory is generally associated with better decision-making quality. From an information and decision-making perspective, individuals possess a variety of views, experiences, knowledge, skills, and cognitive capacities. When these individuals are brought together as a group, their diverse characteristics contribute to enhancing the group’s creativity, quality of thinking, and ability to make better decisions (Bantel & Jackson, <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0014" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>1989</a></span>; Cox & Blake, <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0030" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>1991</a></span>; Jackson, <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0060" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>1992</a></span>; Watson et al., <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0105" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>1993</a></span>). Gender diversity is a key dimension of this heterogeneity, as women directors bring distinct professional experiences, ethical orientations, and behavioral tendencies that help boards evaluate strategic choices and investment opportunities more comprehensively.</p><p>Gender diversity also enhances information exchange among board members. Prior research shows that women directors tend to adopt more cooperative and collaborative approaches and are more oriented toward considering others’ perspectives (Eagly et al., <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0038" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2003</a></span>). They are also more likely to adopt democratic and participative leadership styles rather than autocratic or directive approaches (Eagly & Johnson, <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0039" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>1990</a></span>). These behavioral tendencies promote an open communication environment in which directors are more willing to exchange information and engage in constructive discussion, encouraging the sharing of diverse viewpoints and enhancing information integration (Bantel & Jackson, <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0014" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>1989</a></span>; Eagly et al., <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0038" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2003</a></span>). As a result, gender-diverse boards exhibit richer information exchange and more effective integration of information during discussions, thereby improving board effectiveness and the quality of collective decision-making.</p><p>Furthermore, gender diversity may strengthen the board’s monitoring capabilities. As the body responsible for overseeing managerial behavior and safeguarding shareholder interests (Finkelstein & Hambrick, <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0044" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>1996</a></span>), the board’s monitoring effectiveness is influenced by directors’ psychological traits and behavioral tendencies. Evidence from psychology indicates that women differ from men in personality traits and risk preferences, including greater risk aversion, heightened sensitivity to ethical concerns, and lower competitive and overconfident tendencies (Booth & Nolen, <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0017" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2012</a></span>; Costa et al., <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0029" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2001</a></span>; Jianakoplos & Bernasek, <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0062" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>1998</a></span>; Levi et al., <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0071" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2014</a></span>). These characteristics may improve women directors’ attention on risks and ethical implications and enhance their monitoring capabilities. Consistent with this view, several studies report that women respond differently to men in challenging situations and are better monitors and advisors (Charness & Gneezy, <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0021" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2012</a></span>; Croson & Gneezy, <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0031" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2009</a></span>). For example, Al-Shaer and Zaman (<span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0007" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2016</a></span>) suggest that even when accounting for corporate governance traits and company reporting conditions, a discernible association exists between board gender diversity and higher sustainability reporting quality. Therefore, these findings suggest that gender-diverse boards strengthen the monitoring process and help ensure that managers make effective strategic decisions (Liu, <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0073" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2018</a></span>; Shaukat et al., <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0094" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2016</a></span>; Sun & Zhang, <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0099" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2021</a></span>). Additionally, considering women’s characteristics of risk-aversion and lower confidence, women directors are less likely to take unnecessary risks, which might curb overinvestment behavior (Li & Zeng, <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0072" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2019</a></span>).</p><p>These three mechanisms, greater cognitive diversity, improved information exchange, and stronger monitoring, are likely to be especially relevant in private firms. Prior studies show that, in private firms, financial reporting quality is generally lower (Chen et al., <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0022" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2011</a></span>), public disclosure requirements are minimal (Ball & Shivakumar, <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0013" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2005</a></span>), and external monitoring by financial intermediaries, such as analysts and institutional investors, is limited (Badertscher et al., <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0012" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2013</a></span>). Additionally, ownership tends to be highly concentrated, and shareholders, often owner-managers, are directly involved in operational and strategic decisions (Van Tendeloo & Vanstraelen, <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0104" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2008</a></span>). Chen et al. (<span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0024" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2017</a></span>) find that private firms’ ownership concentration reduces investment efficiency. These characteristics make internal governance mechanisms, especially the composition and diversity of the board, crucial for ensuring high-quality investment decisions. Gender-diverse boards may therefore play a more crucial role in enhancing investment efficiency in private firms. Therefore, we position our hypotheses within this unique context of private firms, arguing that gender-diverse boards improve the quality of strategic and investment decisions, thereby enhancing overall investment efficiency.</p><p>However, the women directors on boards may play different roles in solving overinvestment and underinvestment issues. According to behavioral finance theory, women are significantly more risk averse than men, leading to more cautious investment behavior (Charness & Gneezy, <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0021" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2012</a></span>; Fisher & Yao, <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0045" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2017</a></span>; Jianakoplos & Bernasek, <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0062" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>1998</a></span>). Male directors also engage more frequently in acquisition activities and issue debt, reflecting higher levels of overconfidence (Huang & Kisgen, <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0058" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2013</a></span>). Prior research reports that gender diversity is particularly effective in improving investment efficiency in firms prone to overinvestment (Shin et al., <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0096" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2020</a></span>). Importantly, a significant stream of literature suggests that overinvestment often arises from managerial overconfidence or empire-building incentives (Jensen, <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0061" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>1986</a></span>; Malmendier & Tate, <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0078" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2005</a></span>), which can be mitigated by the greater caution and risk aversion displayed by women directors. In contrast, underinvestment is typically driven by external financing constraints (Machokoto, <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0075" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2021</a></span>; Whited & Wu, <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0106" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2006</a></span>), rather than solely by managerial behavior, board composition has limited ability to mitigate such constraints. Consequently, women directors are more likely to exercise caution when making investment decisions, potentially mitigating overinvestment rather than underinvestment issues. Thus, we formulate the following hypotheses:
</p><div class="quote"><p class="first last">H1a: Women directors are positively associated with investment efficiency in private firms.</p></div>
<div class="quote"><p class="first last">H1b: Women directors are negatively associated with overinvestment, but not with underinvestment in private firms.</p></div><p></p><p class="last">Current studies examine the representation of women directors on corporate boards. They test the underlying hypothesis that board gender diversity is effective when women's attendance reaches a certain percentage. According to tokenism and critical mass theory, women directors who are viewed as ‘tokens’ or ‘symbols’ are more likely to be treated unfairly and have difficulty influencing group decisions unless they reach a critical mass in the firm (Kanter, <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0067" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>1977</a></span>). In other words, boards with only a few women members would experience discriminatory behavior and struggle with decision-making. Empirical evidence generally supports that for women directors to have an impact, there must be a certain level of representation (Cook & Glass, <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0028" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2018</a></span>; Torchia et al., <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0100" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2011</a></span>). Liu et al. (<span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0074" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2014</a></span>) report that when firms have at least three women directors, their contributions become more visible and their suggestions and characteristics are considered, leading to dynamic change in the boardroom and ultimately impacting firm efficiency. In this situation, women members are not regarded as a minority group, and they can communicate their opinions boldly in the firm; thus, they are more likely to make a favorable impact on the management and administration of the firm, reducing conflicts between principals and agents (Yang et al., <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0109" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2019</a></span>). Likewise, Joecks et al. (<span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0065" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2013</a></span>) report that a greater representation of women in the boardroom initially has a detrimental influence on firm performance, but it begins to yield a positive impact once a critical mass of approximately 30% of women members is achieved. Recent evidence, however, shows that even when the proportion of women falls short of a critical mass, the presence of women directors may still be beneficial (Gyapong et al., <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0053" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2016</a></span>; Mcguinness et al., <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0079" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2017</a></span>; Sattar et al., <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0089" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2023</a></span>). Considering that private firms often have smaller boards, their decision-making processes may be more significantly influenced by a single director (Cheng, <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0026" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2008</a></span>). This heightened influence of individual directors on smaller boards underscores the importance of each board member’s contribution. Furthermore, unlike listed firms, UK private firms are not mandated to report gender diversity-related policies, nor are they assessed on their level of diversity. This lack of regulatory pressure indicates that the presence of women directors in UK private firms is not merely a symbolic gesture or a compliance measure. Instead, these women directors are intentionally appointed to leverage their unique perspectives and expertise, thereby contributing to the board’s overall effectiveness and strategy decision-making. Therefore, it seems reasonable that appointing even one woman director to the board could help firms improve their corporate investment efficiency. Considering the characteristic of board size in the sample, and consistent with most studies in the existing literature (Liu et al., <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0074" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2014</a></span>), we classify a board as ‘skewed’, ‘tilted’, or ‘balanced’ when there is only one woman director, two women directors, and at least three women directors, respectively. Nevertheless, given the mixed evidence in the literature, we empirically test whether a critical mass effect exists in the context of UK private firms. The segregation of women director representation forms the basis of the second hypothesis:
</p><div class="quote"><p class="first last">H2a: Private firm investment efficiency is greater given a critical mass of women on the board.</p></div>
<div class="quote"><p class="first last">H2b: Private firm overinvestment is reduced given a critical mass of women on the board, whereas no such effect is expected for underinvestment.</p></div><p></p></div></div><div class="NLM_sec NLM_sec_level_1" id="S003"><h2 class="section-heading-2" id="d1e567">3.  Variable measurement and research design</h2><div class="NLM_sec NLM_sec_level_2" id="S003-S2001"><h3 class="section-heading-3" id="d1e571">3.1.  Variable measurement</h3><p class="last">The proxy for investment efficiency is derived from the model introduced by Biddle et al. (<span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0016" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2009</a></span>). Following prior research examining investment efficiency (Benlemlih & Bitar, <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0015" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2018</a></span>; Jin & Yu, <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0064" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2018</a></span>; Mirza et al., <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0081" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2020</a></span>; Rajkovic, <span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0086" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2020</a></span>), we calculate firm investment because of growth prospects as stated in Equation (1):
<span class="NLM_disp-formula-image disp-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" class="mml-formula" data-formula-source="{"type":"image","src":"/cms/asset/175e6d72-7038-4c68-84b8-a9d75c33f9aa/raie_a_2622079_m0001.gif"}"><span class="mml-formula"><span class="disp_formula_label_div">(1) </span></span></span><span class="NLM_disp-formula disp-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" data-formula-source="{"type":"mathjax"}"><math><mi>IN</mi><msub><mi>V</mi><mrow><mi>i</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>t</mi></mrow></msub><mo>=</mo><msub><mtext fontfamily="times">β</mtext><mn>0</mn></msub><mo>+</mo><mrow><mspace width="thickmathspace"></mspace></mrow><msub><mtext fontfamily="times">β</mtext><mn>1</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">ΔSALE</mi><msub><mi>S</mi><mrow><mi>i</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>t</mi><mo>−</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub><mo>+</mo><msub><mi>ϵ</mi><mrow><mi>i</mi><mo>.</mo><mi>t</mi></mrow></msub></math><span class="mathjaxLabel"><span class="disp_formula_label_div">(1) </span></span></span><span class="NLM_disp-formula-image inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" class="mml-formula" data-formula-source="{"type":"image","src":"/cms/asset/2d6e8f9f-4415-4be2-bd8f-2af913498287/raie_a_2622079_ilm0001.gif"}"></span><span class="NLM_disp-formula inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" data-formula-source="{"type":"mathjax"}"><math><mi>INV</mi></math></span> is the proxy of investments, calculated as the net increase in tangible and intangible assets, multiplied by 100, and scaled by the lagged value of total assets; and <span class="NLM_disp-formula-image inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" class="mml-formula" data-formula-source="{"type":"image","src":"/cms/asset/168878c6-50a9-4e32-8c6b-8c48a7784052/raie_a_2622079_ilm0002.gif"}"></span><span class="NLM_disp-formula inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" data-formula-source="{"type":"mathjax"}"><math><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">ΔSALES</mi></mrow></math></span> is the lagged annual percentage change in sales. More specifically, we estimate the investment value as a function of sales growth for each company using panel data for private firms. The industry classification uses the two-digit UK primary sector code based on at least 20 observations. The residual values obtained from Equation (1) reflect the deviations from the predicted investment level under given growth opportunities each year. The primary dependent variable is investment efficiency, denoted as <span class="NLM_disp-formula-image inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" class="mml-formula" data-formula-source="{"type":"image","src":"/cms/asset/6e351eb1-02ad-4a42-b9b7-e614e5dd01d0/raie_a_2622079_ilm0003.gif"}"></span><span class="NLM_disp-formula inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" data-formula-source="{"type":"mathjax"}"><math><mi>INV</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">_EFF</mi></math></span>. It is defined as the absolute value of the residuals from Equation (1), and then multiplied by minus one, following the framework proposed by Rajkovic (<span class="ref-lnk lazy-ref bibr"><a data-rid="CIT0086" href="#" data-behaviour="toggle-ref" data-ref-type="bibr" data-label="reference"><span class="off-screen">Citation</span>2020</a></span>). It then follows that a higher value of <span class="NLM_disp-formula-image inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" class="mml-formula" data-formula-source="{"type":"image","src":"/cms/asset/7bcf030e-b03b-4af4-b61a-b6760414d8a7/raie_a_2622079_ilm0004.gif"}"></span><span class="NLM_disp-formula inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" data-formula-source="{"type":"mathjax"}"><math><mi>INV</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">_EFF</mi></math></span> signifies that the firm is operating with a higher degree of investment efficiency. Positive residuals proxy for overinvestment (<span class="NLM_disp-formula-image inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" class="mml-formula" data-formula-source="{"type":"image","src":"/cms/asset/59f0d8a9-451c-4bc7-b9e0-5a624de80881/raie_a_2622079_ilm0005.gif"}"></span><span class="NLM_disp-formula inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" data-formula-source="{"type":"mathjax"}"><math><mi>OVER</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">_INV</mi></math></span>), and underinvestment (<span class="NLM_disp-formula-image inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" class="mml-formula" data-formula-source="{"type":"image","src":"/cms/asset/c368982e-abde-4dd3-8857-f2114454b5d9/raie_a_2622079_ilm0006.gif"}"></span><span class="NLM_disp-formula inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" data-formula-source="{"type":"mathjax"}"><math><mi>UNDER</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">_INV</mi></math></span>) is estimated by taking the negative residuals from Equation (1) and multiplying them by minus one. Therefore, the lower value of <span class="NLM_disp-formula-image inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" class="mml-formula" data-formula-source="{"type":"image","src":"/cms/asset/62b37007-0b24-403b-8069-9e749244c75b/raie_a_2622079_ilm0007.gif"}"></span><span class="NLM_disp-formula inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" data-formula-source="{"type":"mathjax"}"><math><mi>OVER</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">_INV</mi><mspace width="thickmathspace"></mspace></math></span>(<span class="NLM_disp-formula-image inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" class="mml-formula" data-formula-source="{"type":"image","src":"/cms/asset/d0b0a7c5-0eba-4159-9a24-f5d7b34dd715/raie_a_2622079_ilm0008.gif"}"></span><span class="NLM_disp-formula inline-formula rs_preserve"><img src="//:0" alt="" data-formula-source="{"type":"mathjax"}"><math><mi>UNDER</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">_INV</mi></math></span>), the fewer overinvestment (underinvestment) issues.</p></div><div class="NLM_sec NLM_sec_level_2" id="S003-S2002"><h3 class="section-heading-3" id="d1e715">3.2.  Empirical model</h3><p>The association between board gender diversity and the efficiency of corporate investments is stated in Equation (2):
